rationale_predicted: The skill is a CLI wrapper around Chrome/Chromium via CDP for browser automation, so it will spawn the agent-browser binary (or via npx) and launch a browser subprocess. Browser automation implies arbitrary network access to any user-specified site, plus screenshot/state writes to disk and reads from session/auth vault config. Installation via npm and the 'skills get' subcommands suggest reading/writing local config and cache directories. Path and host predicates are broad because the skill's verbs (navigate, screenshot, scrape) target arbitrary user-chosen URLs and local artifact paths.

The skill detects the project's framework by reading package manifests (package.json, requirements.txt, angular.json, etc.) via cat/ls/grep, then installs the Auth0 CLI via brew and runs `auth0 login`/`auth0 apps create` which authenticate against Auth0 hosted endpoints. Credentials returned by the CLI are typically written to a local environment file. The reference docs (references/cli.md, references/environments.md, references/concepts.md) are read from the skill bundle, and external Auth0 docs/community sites are linked for Tier 2 frameworks.

The skill orchestrates pre-deployment validation by reading and updating .azure/deployment-plan.md and reviewing infrastructure-as-code (Bicep/Terraform) plus azure.yaml. It explicitly invokes commands like 'azd provision --preview', 'bicep build', and 'terraform validate', which spawn azd/bicep/terraform/az subprocesses; build verification adds language-specific build tools (node/npm/dotnet/python). These azd/az/terraform CLI calls authenticate and query Azure ARM/Graph endpoints, hence the management.azure.com, login.microsoftonline.com, and graph.microsoft.com hosts; mcr.microsoft.com may be hit when pulling Bicep types/container images.

Caveman is a pure communication-style skill that only modifies how the agent phrases its responses. It contains no instructions to read/write files, make network requests, or invoke subprocesses. The skill's entire scope is prose compression rules and intensity levels, with no IO surface area.

The skill is centered on writing CloudFormation templates (YAML/JSON) and invoking the AWS CLI for stack operations, so the agent will read/write template files in the working directory and shell out to the `aws` binary. AWS CLI calls reach CloudFormation regional endpoints under amazonaws.com and rely on credentials at ~/.aws/. No deletions of local files are implied — `delete-stack` operates on remote AWS resources, not local filesystem paths.

The skill installs npm dependencies (npm install reads/writes node_modules and package-lock.json, fetching from registry.npmjs.org) and runs a Node.js script that connects to Chrome's DevTools Protocol over a local WebSocket (127.0.0.1:9222) to read cookies. It then uploads those cookies to Browserbase via their API, requiring network access to browserbase.com endpoints. The follow-up workflow invokes the `browse` CLI subprocess for authenticated sessions, and Chrome itself may be launched with debugging flags using a /tmp/chrome-debug user data dir.

The skill's primary verbs are searching for and installing skills via `npx skills find/add`, which spawns npx/node subprocesses. Installing skills writes to a local or global skills directory and pulls package metadata from npm and skill source files from GitHub, while the leaderboard reference at skills.sh implies network calls to that host. No deletions are implied since the skill only adds and updates skills.

The skill centers on Firebase Hosting Classic, which requires reading/writing firebase.json and .firebaserc config and a public build directory, then invoking the firebase CLI via npx to deploy or run local emulators. Network calls go to npm registry to fetch firebase-tools and to Google/Firebase APIs for auth and deploy. The emulator binds localhost:5000 for serving. No deletions are implied by the documented verbs (configure, deploy, emulate).

This skill is an auditor that reads Firestore security rules files (typically firestore.rules) and produces a JSON assessment as its return value. It performs no writes, deletions, network calls, or subprocess invocations — it is purely an analysis/scoring prompt. The only IO is reading the rules file(s) and likely the firebase.json config to locate them.

The skill invokes the firecrawl CLI (or npx firecrawl) to scrape arbitrary user-supplied URLs, so it spawns the firecrawl binary and connects to api.firecrawl.dev plus any third-party host the user wants scraped (effectively unrestricted). Output is written to ./.firecrawl/ files per the documented naming convention, and the tips suggest reading those files back with grep/head. No deletions are described.

This skill builds frontend code (HTML/CSS/JS, React, Vue), so the agent will read existing project files and write new component/page files in typical frontend locations. It will likely fetch fonts (the skill emphasizes distinctive typography, implying Google Fonts or similar) and may install packages (Motion library is explicitly mentioned for React) via npm/npx. No deletions are implied by the design-focused verbs. Subprocess invocations cover Node-based tooling commonly used to scaffold and run frontend projects.

This skill is a static security review of GitHub Actions workflows performed entirely via local file reading and grep-based pattern matching. It explicitly lists files to review (.github/workflows/*.yml, action.yml, local composite actions, CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md/Makefile/shell scripts) and references (references/*.md) that the agent loads selectively. The allowed-tools are Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Task — no network or write operations are implied; Bash is likely used only for read-only inspection commands like grep/find/git. No outputs are written to disk and no external hosts are contacted.

This is primarily a conversational/interviewing skill with no declared external integrations. The only IO implied is codebase exploration ('If a question can be answered by exploring the codebase, explore the codebase instead'), which means filesystem reads of project files via Read/Grep/Glob. No writes, deletions, network calls, or subprocesses are implied by the prose.

The skill walks the codebase to find architectural friction, so it reads broadly across project files plus the domain glossary (CONTEXT.md), ADRs in docs/adr/, and the bundled LANGUAGE.md/INTERFACE-DESIGN.md references. During the grilling loop it lazily creates or updates CONTEXT.md and may write new ADRs under docs/adr/. It does no network I/O and spawns no external binaries — exploration is delegated to the Explore subagent (in-process), and all side effects are local file edits.

This skill is a pure reference document for the Pinecone MCP server tools. The agent invokes these tools through the MCP protocol (already-running server), not by spawning subprocesses or touching local files. Network traffic ultimately reaches Pinecone's API endpoints (api.pinecone.io and related *.pinecone.io hosts) via the MCP server. No filesystem reads/writes/deletes are implied by the documented verbs.

The skill provisions a Prisma Postgres database via curl calls to api.prisma.io, then configures a local Node.js project. It reads/writes .env, .gitignore, prisma/schema.prisma, prisma.config.ts and package.json, installs npm packages (hitting registry.npmjs.org), and runs npx prisma migrate/generate which writes prisma/migrations and generated client files. Step 7 explicitly creates and then deletes test-connection.ts after running it via npx tsx, and references/*.md files are read for guidance.

This skill is pure prose documentation describing prompting techniques for image generation models. It contains no executable instructions, no CLI commands, no API endpoints to call directly, and no file paths to read or write. It references sibling skills (find-models, compare-models) but does not itself perform any IO. A representative task exercising the primary verb (writing prompts) would only produce prompt text, not trigger filesystem, network, or subprocess activity.

This skill is primarily a prose reference guide containing 70 React/Next.js performance rules organized by priority. When applied to a representative refactoring or code-review task, the agent will read the skill's own rule files (rules/*.md, AGENTS.md) plus the user's React/Next.js source files, then write modifications back to those source files. The skill itself declares no network endpoints, no CLI tools, and no deletion patterns—it's pure guidance applied to existing code, so subprocess/network/deletion footprints are empty.

The skill primarily runs the semgrep CLI to scan code (reading source files across the project) and emits findings to stdout or output files like results.sarif/results.json. Rule creation involves writing YAML rule files and test files in a <rule-id>/ directory structure. Semgrep fetches rulesets like p/security-audit and p/owasp-top-ten from semgrep.dev's registry over the network, and installation may invoke pip, brew, or docker. Local references under references/ are also read.

The skill instructs the agent to detect installed AI SDKs by grepping package.json/requirements.txt/pyproject.toml, then write Sentry init code into JS/TS/Python config files (e.g., sentry.edge.config.ts). It may install Sentry SDK packages via npm or pip, which contacts npmjs.org/pypi.org. The skill explicitly tells the agent to verify against docs.sentry.io. No deletions are implied — only configuration additions.

This skill orchestrates a full skill-creation lifecycle: drafting SKILL.md, snapshotting skills with cp, running eval subagents, aggregating benchmarks via python scripts, launching an HTML viewer with nohup python, and packaging into .skill files. It reads/writes the skill directory, evals JSON, and a workspace tree organized by iteration and eval ID. Network egress is implied by the description-optimization loop which invokes `claude -p` (hitting api.anthropic.com) to evaluate trigger queries. Subprocess footprint includes python (aggregation, viewer, packaging, optimization loop), cp (snapshotting), kill (viewer teardown), open (HTML preview), and claude CLI.

This skill instructs the agent to install @vercel/sandbox via pnpm and write TypeScript code that creates Vercel Sandbox microVMs, so it will read/write package.json, node_modules, and likely vercel.json for cron config. It needs Vercel API credentials (VERCEL_TOKEN/TEAM/PROJECT) read from env, and reaches api.vercel.com to provision sandboxes plus npm registry for installs. Inside the host process the agent shells out to pnpm/npm/npx/node/tsx (and references sh, sudo, dnf, ldconfig, base64, agent-browser as commands run inside the sandbox VM, which still appear as subprocess invocations from the SDK perspective). Browser automation targets arbitrary URLs (example.com is the documented target).

The skill drives the inference.sh `belt` CLI to call Tavily and Exa search/extract apps, so the agent will spawn `belt` subprocesses (and possibly `npx` for the related-skills install commands). Workflows redirect output to local JSON files like search_results.json and content.json, implying writes (and subsequent reads) of relative .json files. Network egress goes to inference.sh's cloud endpoints, which proxy to Tavily and Exa providers, plus openrouter.ai for the LLM steps shown in the workflow examples.

The wrangler skill is a CLI wrapper that primarily spawns the wrangler binary (via npm/npx) for Cloudflare Workers operations. It reads/writes wrangler.jsonc config, generates TypeScript types, manages migrations, and uses local state in .wrangler/state. It connects to Cloudflare APIs for deployment, KV/R2/D1/Vectorize operations, and fetches docs from developers.cloudflare.com. Container operations imply docker subprocess, and npm install is needed to bootstrap wrangler itself.

The skill drives a bundled Swift package via `swift run`, which compiles and executes a Swift tool that reads and rewrites the `.pbxproj` file and links plist resources, so the Xcode project files are both read and written. SPM resolution fetches packages (Alamofire, Firebase iOS SDK) over the network, primarily from github.com via git, and Swift toolchain caches land under `~/Library/Caches` and DerivedData. The skill explicitly mandates running `swift --version` (and suggests `xcode-select --install`), and SPM internally invokes `git`, hence the subprocess set. No deletions are described; the script is idempotent and additive only.
